mnemonics.title: Checksum Calculation Steps
mnemonics.content: COPS: Complement Ones sum, Put in field, Send with data. Receiver: Sum all including checksum, Should get all 1s.

mnemonics.title: Remember Internet Checksum Rule
mnemonics.content: DIVAS: Divide data into 16-bit words, Invert bits, Verify by summing (all 1s = good), Add carry back, Send checksum.

mnemonics.title: Ones Complement Key Point
mnemonics.content: ONE RULE: Overflow is ONE moRe (end-aRound carry), NOT discarded.

acronyms.usage: More powerful error detection than checksum, used in Ethernet, ZIP files
Layer 3 protocol that uses 16-bit header checksum in IPv4
Parity check across columns of data, predecessor to checksum
Transport layer protocol using optional checksum in IPv4, mandatory in IPv6
Reliable transport protocol using 16-bit checksum for error detection

visualTips.visualization: Draw a circle for modular arithmetic. Show carry wrapping around from MSB to LSB with an arrow.
Draw a box: [Data Words] + [Checksum Field] → [Adder] → [All 1s?]. YES = Valid, NO = Error.
Draw IP header rows, highlight Source IP, Dest IP, Protocol, TTL, and Checksum field in different color as NOT included.
0→1, 1→0. Show: 10110010 → 01001101. Simple inversion of all bits.

analogies.analogy: Like fingerprints identify people, checksum identifies data. If data changes, fingerprint (checksum) changes.
Like comparing two copies of a document by counting words on each page - mismatch means error.
Think of a car odometer rolling over from 99999 to 00000 - the overflow '1' comes back to front.
Like a checksum digit on credit cards - catches transcription errors but not all fraud.
